page: Microsoft Azure HB368-288rs_v5 is an x86_64 architecture server instance belonging to the HBv5 family. It features 288 dedicated vCPUs powered by Intel processors and runs on the Microsoft Hyper-V hypervisor. The system is equipped with 448.0 GB of system memory, resulting in a resource density of 1.56 GB of RAM per core.

This instance includes 15,851 GB of local storage and supports Premium Storage. It is RDMA (Remote Direct Memory Access) capable, facilitating low-latency, high-throughput networking across clusters. No complimentary public IPv4 addresses are included with this configuration.

The hardware profile presents a clear tradeoff between massive parallel compute capacity and memory allocation per core. With only 1.56 GB of RAM per vCPU, memory-bound workloads may face bottlenecks, whereas compute-bound tasks benefit from the high density of 288 dedicated physical or virtual cores. From a cost-efficiency perspective, the high density of compute resources and large local storage capacity reduce the need for multi-node overhead in scenarios that require high-core counts but moderate memory footprints.

This server type is designed for high-performance computing (HPC) workloads, large-scale parallel simulations, computational fluid dynamics, and storage-heavy database operations that require rapid local disk access and high-core execution without massive per-core memory requirements.
description: Microsoft Azure HB368-288rs_v5 is an x86_64 compute-optimized server instance within the HBv5 family, running on the Microsoft Hyper-V hypervisor. It features 288 dedicated vCPUs powered by Intel processors, paired with 448.0 GB of system memory, which provides a low memory-to-core ratio of 1.56 GB per vCPU. The instance is bundled with 15,851 GB of local storage and supports Premium Storage alongside RDMA networking capabilities. It does not include any complimentary public IPv4 addresses or dedicated GPUs. This configuration presents a tradeoff, offering massive parallel processing power and substantial local storage capacity, but limited memory allocation per core. It is suited for high-performance computing, parallel simulations, and storage-intensive database workloads that do not require high per-core memory density.
